title: The State of Text-to-Video Today
concept: Text-to-video is rapidly improving but remains more limited in length, resolution, and consistency compared to mature text-to-image systems. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Audio and Music Generation.'

exercises.prompt: Which is harder to make with AI: a single picture, or a 10-second video?

options.text: They are exactly the same difficulty

exercises.explain: A video is basically hundreds of pictures that all need to connect smoothly and stay consistent — that's much trickier than making just one image!

cards.title: Building a Bridge to Video
cards.body: AI got really good at making still pictures. Now it's building a bridge to something harder: moving pictures! But that bridge isn't finished yet — text-to-video is still under construction.

cards.title: Text-to-Video Today
cards.body: Text-to-video AI can turn written prompts into short video clips. It's improving fast, but compared to text-to-image, it still struggles with longer clips, sharp resolution, and keeping things looking consistent from frame to frame.

cards.title: Like a Flipbook Artist
cards.body: Imagine an artist who can draw one perfect picture easily. Now ask them to draw hundreds of pictures in a row that all flow together into a smooth flipbook animation. That's way harder — and sometimes a page or two looks a little off. That's where text-to-video AI is right now.

cards.title: A Wobbly Walk
cards.body: You type: 'A dog runs across a beach.' The AI makes a short clip — but halfway through, the dog's legs might glitch, or its color might shift slightly. Text-to-image wouldn't have this problem because there's only one moment to get right, not hundreds in a row.

cards.title: Myth: Video AI Is Just as Good as Image AI
cards.body: Nope! Many people assume since AI images look amazing, AI videos must be just as perfect. In reality, video AI is still catching up — clips are often shorter, blurrier, and less consistent than a single sharp AI image.

cards.title: Image AI vs Video AI
cards.body: Comparing where each type of AI stands today.

left.text: Mature, sharp, detailed, one consistent moment to render

right.text: Still developing, shorter clips, lower resolution, harder to stay consistent

exercises.prompt: Compared to text-to-image AI, text-to-video AI is generally:

options.text: More advanced and always higher resolution
Still catching up, with shorter clips and less consistency
Exactly the same in every way

exercises.explain: Text-to-video is improving quickly, but it still lags behind text-to-image in length, resolution, and consistency.

exercises.prompt: True or False: AI-generated videos today are usually just as long and detailed as full movies.
exercises.explain: False! Most AI-generated videos today are still short clips, not full-length, highly detailed movies.

exercises.sentence: Text-to-video AI often struggles to keep things looking ___ from one frame to the next.
exercises.explain: Keeping people, objects, and backgrounds looking the same across many frames (consistency) is one of the biggest challenges for video AI.

exercises.prompt: Why is making an AI video harder than making an AI image?

options.text: Because a video needs many connected frames to look smooth, not just one
Because videos don't use any AI models at all
Because images require more computer memory than videos

exercises.explain: A video is made of many frames that must flow together smoothly, which is much harder than perfecting a single still image.

exercises.prompt: A friend says, 'AI video generators are basically perfect now, just like AI image generators.' What's the best response, and what's coming up next in our AI journey?

options.text: That's true, video AI has no limitations left; next we'll learn about robots
Actually, video AI is still improving and has limits in length, resolution, and consistency; next up is Audio and Music Generation
Video AI is worse than it was five years ago; next we'll learn about coding
Image AI and video AI were invented at the same time and are equally advanced; next is video games

exercises.explain: Text-to-video is rapidly improving but still has real limits compared to mature text-to-image systems — shorter clips, lower resolution, and consistency issues. Next up: Audio and Music Generation.
